title: Explaining the Mermaid Diagram Support Diff
summary: This walkthrough explains the change that lets explain-html-gen accept validated Mermaid flowchart source, render it as an offline tabbed diagram section, and reject unsupported Mermaid before HTML generation.
author: explain-diff-html

background.intro: The generator turns structured JSON into one self-contained HTML page. Before this change, the schema had a diagrams field, but the builder did not render diagram content and there was no Mermaid-specific validation path.
background.mental_model: Think of Mermaid support as a gated adapter: JSON supplies Mermaid source, schema validation proves the source is in the supported subset, and the HTML builder renders a local diagram without loading Mermaid JavaScript from the network.

components.name: Mermaid parser package
components.description: The new pkg/mermaid package accepts a small flowchart subset and reports line-specific errors for unsupported syntax.

components.name: Generator input schema
components.description: The Diagram struct now has a mermaid source field, and GeneratorInput.Validate calls the Mermaid validator for diagrams of type mermaid.

components.name: HTML builder
components.description: The builder adds a Diagrams section, tab controls, local diagram markup, responsive CSS, and JavaScript for switching tabs.

background.prior: Previously, Mermaid text could not be represented as a first-class diagram type. The generator had no way to validate Mermaid before rendering, and a skill could not get a precise error to repair invalid diagram source.

intuition.intro: The change keeps the generated page offline-first while still allowing a skill to express architecture as Mermaid.
intuition.core_idea: Accept only the Mermaid subset the Go code can understand, fail fast when the source is outside that subset, and render the accepted structure with local HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
intuition.old_behavior: Diagram input was effectively dormant for Mermaid use: a skill could mention diagrams, but this generator did not validate or display Mermaid as a rendered page section.
intuition.new_behavior: A JSON diagram with type mermaid is parsed during input validation. Valid diagrams appear in a tabbed Diagrams section; invalid diagrams stop the CLI with an actionable validation error.
intuition.trade_offs_edge_cases: This is not full Mermaid compatibility. Subgraphs, custom class definitions, click handlers, and style directives are rejected because the local renderer cannot safely or accurately reproduce them without a Mermaid runtime.

code.intro: The implementation splits responsibility cleanly: parsing lives in pkg/mermaid, schema validation invokes it, and the builder handles presentation.

subsections.title: Mermaid Validation Boundary
subsections.explanation: The parser accepts graph or flowchart headers, common directions, labeled nodes, and labeled edges. Unsupported Mermaid lines return a precise error instead of being rendered incorrectly.

blocks.caption: The public Validate function is intentionally small, so callers get the same parsing rules the renderer uses.

blocks.code:
func unsupportedMermaidLine(line string) bool {
    lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(line))
    return strings.HasPrefix(lower, "subgraph ") ||
        lower == "end" ||
        strings.Contains(lower, ":::") ||
        strings.Contains(lower, "click ") ||
        strings.Contains(lower, "classdef ") ||
        strings.Contains(lower, "style ")
}
blocks.caption: The validator rejects syntax the local renderer does not claim to support.

subsections.consequences: Skills can feed the CLI stderr back into a repair prompt when Mermaid is invalid, rather than discovering a broken diagram after page generation.

subsections.title: Schema-Level Rejection
subsections.explanation: Validation happens before HTML generation, so invalid Mermaid cannot leak into a partial or misleading artifact.

blocks.caption: The error includes the diagram number and the parser error, which is enough context for an automated retry.

subsections.consequences: The CLI exits nonzero for unsupported Mermaid, giving the calling skill a deterministic repair signal.

subsections.title: Tabbed Offline Rendering
subsections.explanation: The builder inserts a Diagrams section only when diagrams are present, then renders each diagram as a tab panel with local markup and a source disclosure.

blocks.caption: The new section is conditional, so older examples without diagrams keep the original page shape.

subsections.consequences: Generated pages remain self-contained: the Mermaid view is rendered by this generator, not by a CDN or browser-side package.

quiz.question: Why does the change validate Mermaid before HTML generation begins?
options: So unsupported syntax can fail fast with an error the skill can use for repair
So Mermaid JavaScript can be downloaded before the page is opened
So the CLI can skip normal JSON validation for diagram-heavy inputs
So all diagrams are converted into PNG files before writing HTML
quiz.explanation: Validation runs during GeneratorInput.Validate, before builder output. That gives the caller a precise failure instead of a misleading rendered page.

quiz.question: Which Mermaid feature is intentionally rejected by the new parser?
options: A graph TD or flowchart LR header
A simple labeled edge such as A -->|calls| B
A node label written as API[HTTP API]
A subgraph block with an end marker
quiz.explanation: Subgraph syntax is outside the supported local-renderer subset, so the parser rejects lines starting with subgraph and the matching end line.

quiz.question: What keeps the generated HTML offline-first even with Mermaid input?
options: The page links to Mermaid only when the browser already has it cached
The Go builder renders accepted Mermaid as local HTML and CSS markup
The validator strips diagrams from the page after checking them
The CLI embeds a base64 copy of the Mermaid npm package
quiz.explanation: The builder parses the diagram and emits local markup, CSS, and small tab JavaScript. It does not depend on Mermaid JS or any external resource.

quiz.question: What information does a Mermaid validation failure include?
options: Only a generic statement that the generated HTML is invalid
The browser console stack trace from Mermaid's renderer
The diagram number plus the parser's specific source problem
A complete rewritten Mermaid diagram suggested by the Go package
quiz.explanation: Schema validation wraps parser errors as messages like diagram 1 has invalid mermaid source: unsupported mermaid syntax on line 2.

quiz.question: Why does the table of contents add the Diagrams link conditionally?
options: Because older inputs without diagrams should not link to a missing section
Because diagrams are hidden until the quiz has been completed
Because the HTML validator requires exactly three table-of-contents links
Because the browser loads tab JavaScript only after a link is clicked
quiz.explanation: The builder only creates the Diagrams section when the input includes diagrams, so the TOC link is added under the same condition.

diagrams.title: Mermaid Diff Flow
diagrams.caption: How Mermaid source moves from JSON input to validated offline HTML rendering.
diagrams.mermaid:
flowchart LR
Input[Generator Input] --> Validate[Mermaid Validate]
Validate --> Build[HTML Builder]
Build --> Tabs[Diagram Tabs]
Build --> Source[Source Details]
